[What's the SECRET to Jordan The Stallion's 13M TikTok Followers?](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HNHbv3ghvw8)

•

•

36,555 views 1 year ago

In this episode of Creator Spotlight, host Shira Lazar interviews Jordan Howlett (aka @Jordan_The_Stallion8) TikTok sensation and president of the Fast Food Secrets Club. Together, they explore the nonlinear journey that took Jordan from Division 1 baseball player to over 13 million followers in TikTok. Learn the unique way he works with brands as Jordan shares the core philosophies that help him stay productive, ambitious and productive; including the roles that faith, family and fun play in each. Discover how a commitment to authenticity and bold moves led him to collaborations with Kevin Hart, Donald Glover, Halle Berry and Method Man just to name a few. About Creator Spotlight Creator Spotlight is powered by PRophet and What’s Trending. PRophet is an award-winning, AI-powered communications platform designed to help communicators engage with top-tier journalists, influencers, and industry leaders. CREATORS 08:03 - FROM BASEBALL TO SOCIAL MEDIA 10:28 - PRODUCTION PROCESS 11:27 - METHOD MAN AND CELEBRITIES 14:07 - FAST FOOD SECRETS CLUB 15:05 - NONLINEAR SUCCESS 15:40 - THE FUTURE FOR JORDAN •[3 days ago](https://www.youtube.com/post/UgkxDz2gq_5UILQ3ZGrcyx7r7Dxml3k1e5YD)

🚨 Epic Games has reportedly confirmed that Fortnite players can now receive refunds for all cosmetics tied to the artist D4vd, including popular emotes like "Feel It" and "Trophy Drop." This decision follows serious criminal charges against the 21-year-old singer, David Anthony Burke, involving the murder of a 14-year-old girl. The community had been calling for the removal of these items for some time, expressing a desire to distance their in-game accounts from the artist's work.⁠ ⁠ Currently, players must manually contact Epic Player Support to request their V-Bucks back. However, starting Tuesday, April 28, the company will launch an automated self-service refund option specifically for these items. This update aims to streamline the process for anyone who purchased the emotes or Jam Tracks, such as "What Are You Waiting For" and "Locked and Loaded," making it easier for the player base to return the content immediately.⁠ ⁠ Epic Games also signaled that this is only the beginning, stating that a "bunch of changes" will be rolled out over time. While they have not yet confirmed if the items will be permanently scrubbed from the game, many players are pushing for the total removal of these cosmetics from all lockers, including those earned through tournaments. ⁠ ⁠ The ongoing rollout suggests that Epic is actively evaluating how to handle the presence of the artist's content within the Fortnite ecosystem moving forward.⁠ ⁠ Link in bio to catch up on the artists arrest. ⁠ ⁠ [#Fortnight](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/fortnight)[#D4vd](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/d4vd)[#WhatsTrending](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/whatstrending)

•[2 weeks ago](https://www.youtube.com/post/UgkxjKg32R2ADca7whxsBS7ucz6tSfZrMbNd)

Actor Ruby Rose has publicly accused singer Katy Perry of sexual assault, stemming from an incident allegedly occurring nearly two decades ago. ⁠ ⁠ Rose shared the allegations via the social media platform Threads, claiming the encounter took place at the Spice Market nightclub in Melbourne when she was in her early 20s. According to Rose, she previously framed the event as a humorous anecdote but has now decided to speak openly about the trauma she experienced.⁠ ⁠ Rose provided specific details of the alleged assault, claiming that Perry approached her while she was resting and engaged in non-consensual physical contact. The actor alleged that she immediately vomited following the incident and kept the matter secret for years. Separately stating Perry later helped assist with her U.S. visa process. Rose further stated that the incident was witnessed by others and that she possesses photographic evidence to support her claims.⁠ ⁠ The timing of these public statements follows Rose’s long-standing criticism of Perry, including a 2017 dispute regarding Perry’s music and professional conduct. Reports state, Rose recently indicated that she has officially taken the matter to authorities, claiming in a social media post that she had just left a police station. ⁠ ⁠ As of the time of the report, Katy Perry's team is pushing back, calling these allegations "categorically false" and "dangerous reckless lies."

•[2 weeks ago](https://www.youtube.com/post/Ugkx7Qx5LItuT9L1vXjz88Qwlas1wLNmjhFr)

During her headlining performance at Coachella 2026, Sabrina Carpenter reportedly faced criticism for her onstage reaction to a "Zaghrouta," a traditional Middle Eastern and North African vocal trill used to express joy. ⁠ ⁠ While seated at her piano, Carpenter appeared confused by the high-pitched sound, labeling it "weird" and jokingly asking if it was a "yodel" or related to Burning Man. Her comments sparked immediate backlash online, with some social media users accusing the pop star of being insensitive and dismissive toward a cultural expression of celebration.⁠ ⁠ In response to the controversy, Carpenter issued an apology on Saturday via social media, explaining that her reaction was rooted in "pure confusion" rather than ill intent. She noted that she could not see the specific crowd member or hear the sound clearly from the stage, leading to her sarcastic remarks. ⁠ ⁠ Acknowledging that she "could have handled it better," the singer expressed her newfound understanding of what a Zaghrouta is and stated that she now welcomes all forms of cheers and celebratory chants from her audience.⁠ .⁠ .⁠ .⁠ [#SabrinaCarpenter](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/sabrinacarpenter)

The "Oppenheimer of AI" just had his darkest week yet. 📉🔥⁠ ⁠ Sam Altman’s world is closing in. Early Friday morning, a suspect was reportedly arrested after allegedly throwing a Molotov cocktail at Altman’s San Francisco home. While the damage was minimal, the message was clear, especially since the same individual reportedly made credible threats against OpenAI’s HQ.⁠ ⁠ But the chaos doesn't stop at physical threats:⁠ ⁠ A bombshell report claims engineers are frustrated that Altman allegedly struggles with basic AI terminology and prioritizes fundraising over actual science.⁠ ⁠ This is happening while State officials are probing ChatGPT’s alleged role in planning a fatal mass shooting at FSU last year.⁠ ⁠ Plus, Elon Musk is officially seeking a court order to strip Altman of his power, alleging he defrauded the mission of OpenAI.⁠ .⁠ .⁠ .⁠ .⁠ .⁠ [#SamAltman](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/samaltman)[#OpenAI](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/openai)[#ChatGPT](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/chatgpt)[#WhatsTrending](https://www.youtube.com/hashtag/whatstrending)

R&B icons Chris Brown and Usher have officially announced their "Raymond & Brown (R&B)" joint tour, sparking massive excitement across social media with teaser posts garnering hundreds of thousands of likes within hours. ⁠ ⁠ This milestone collaboration effectively silences past rumors of friction, leaning into a long-standing dynamic that fans have anticipated since Usher first teased the potential for a joint trek in 2024. While specific dates and ticket information are still under wraps, industry analysts expect the tour to break sales records and stadium attendance marks, fueled by the combined power of two loyal fanbases and Brown’s recent history of selling out massive venues globally.⁠ ⁠ This "Raymond & Brown" event is reportedly set to be a generational crossover, blending legendary vocals with the complex choreography both stars are known for. Jasveen Sangha, the North Hollywood woman known as the “Ketamine Queen,” has been sentenced to 15 years in federal prison for her role in the death of actor Matthew Perry. Sangha pleaded guilty to multiple charges, admitting to running a high-volume drug trafficking operation out of her home and supplying the ketamine that led to Perry’s fatal overdose in 2023. During the sentencing, U.S. District Judge Sherilyn Peace Garnett reporedtedly identified Sangha as a primary figure in the conspiracy, noting she was "probably one of the most culpable" among the five defendants charged. Reports state Sangha covered her mouth as she was handed her sentence, blowing a kiss to her family in the gallery as she left. ## Your Privacy Choices: Opt-out of sale or sharing of personal information and opt out of targeted ads

When you interact with us or our services, we may disclose personal information to third parties, partners, and NBCUniversal’s [Related Businesses](https://corporate.comcast.com/privacy-related-businesses) as described in our [Privacy Policy](https://www.nbcuniversalprivacy.com/privacy#accordionheader5). Some of this activity may be considered a "sale" or "share” under applicable law (residents of applicable states can learn more [here](https://www.nbcuniversalprivacy.com/privacy#accordionheader5)). To opt out of such “sale” or “sharing,” follow the steps below. Opting out will also prevent the processing of your personal information for targeted ads.

*   For direct contact information such as your name, email address and other associated personal information, please submit the form below first. 
*   For cookies and devices identifiers: switch the “Allow Sale/Share/Targeted Ads” toggle under Manage Preferences to OFF (grey color) by moving it LEFT and clicking “Confirm My Choice.” You must log in to your account first if you want your choice to apply across browsers and devices that you access through your account.**This will close this box, so please open the Opt-Out Form first.**

**Please note:**Opting out will also prevent disclosure of personal information to our [Related Businesses](https://corporate.comcast.com/privacy-related-businesses) (except as necessary for the provision of services or when you permit to such disclosure).

You will still see ads, but they may be less relevant or based only on our first-party information about you.

**Your choices related to cookies and device identifiers will apply to devices accessed with your logged in account if you opt out (or opt back in) when you are logged in.** If you are not logged in, your choice will only apply to this browser, and you will need to renew this choice on each browser or device where you want your choice to apply or if you clear your browser cookies.

# The Key Spring 2026 Trends to Add to Your Wardrobe Now

By [Andrea Zendejas](/contributor/andrea-zendejas)

March 19, 2026

Photo: Courtesy of Kallmeyer, Bottega Veneta (ImaxTree), Chanel, Maria McManus, Boss, Loewe, Khaite, Gorunway.com

Save StorySave this story

Save StorySave this story

The spring 2026 collections arrived with a palpable sense of excitement, and for a good reason. This season marked a true industry reset, with nearly 15 newly appointed creative directors debuting their visions and setting the tone for the spring 2026 trends across the major fashion houses. The overarching sentiment was expressive, celebratory, and deeply personal—driven by individuality and the pure joy of getting dressed.

One of the season’s leading themes was “fashion as feeling”—a fitting sentiment not only captured in the movement of the clothes (the fringe! the feathers! the frothy lace!) as designers experimented with tactile textures and proportion, but also the emotional finale of Mathieu Blazy’s Chanel debut, when Awar Odhiang closed the show with a smile so bright it lit up the entire Grand Palais.

A certain kind of soft, relaxed elegance was also explored this season, characterized by scarf styling and drop-waist silhouettes for an overall look that felt sophisticated yet undone. Prep’s moment continues, with an emphasis on color and practical layering that feels apt for transitional dressing and a little more expressive than your traditional uniform.

The spring 2026 collections pick up where we left off in the fall, with personality-driven looks rooted in wearability. Think of it as real-life dressing with a flourish. Find an introduction to the key spring 2026 trends below—with the key pieces to shop to recreate your favorite moods, as the collections trickle into stores.

*Want even more trends? Make sure to check out our guide to the [top spring shoe trends](https://www.vogue.com/article/spring-2026-shoe-trends) and the key [spring handbag trends](https://www.vogue.com/article/spring-2026-handbag-trends).*

Photos: Courtesy of Burberry, Akris, Lemaire, Balmain, Ralph Lauren

## Utility Dressing

Utility dressing answers the call for clothes that work as hard as you do. Designers leaned into uniform-inspired silhouettes made from durable fabrics, with details rooted in functionality. Earth tones define the palette, however, their sensible nature doesn’t mean boring—especially not when paired with something unexpected for contrast (see: Burberry’s metallic tunic and cargo jacket mashup).

## Recreation Dressing

From the runway to real life, this season’s fashion took cues from the hobbies and pastimes that shape our everyday routines. Lifestyle staples became standout fashion moments, from Celine’s riding helmets and Miu Miu’s aprons to Dries Van Noten’s board shorts. The result is a wardrobe that feels personal and rooted in the pleasure of daily life.

## Retro Summer

Planning your next holiday in the sun? Look to retro-inspired prints to bring a nostalgic charm to your vacation wardrobe. Channel the mood with Suzie Kondi’s striped trousers, Chloé’s floral bodysuit, or Dries Van Noten’s kaftan pieces that feel equal parts glamorous, relaxed, and ready for an escape.

## Mode Sportif

Nylon anoraks and technical bottoms styled with heels and tailoring prove that the contrast is key when fashion-ifying sporty staples. Prada shows us how it's done by pairing a citron-hued snap jacket with crisp shirting and a full skirt.

## Jazz Age

A silhouette favored by icons both fictional and real, the 1920s shift dress returns in modern fabrics and prints. Its drop-waist and relaxed, midi-length fit offers an elegant alternative to tailoring or overly draped pieces; its inherent ease perfectly aligns with the season’s softer approach.

## The Art of the In-Between

When the weather can’t make up its mind, it’s time to get ultra-creative with your layers. Out-style mercurial forecasts with clever—and lightweight—pieces that offer warmth without the bulk. In practice, that might look like colorful shirting paired with cropped outerwear and a silk scarf (à la Miu Miu or Bottega); or a cropped jacket atop a button-down and knit.

## Literary Chic

Fans of fall’s modern prep look will find much to love in spring’s take, as designers pushed the idea of uniform dressing into more expressive territory with offbeat color combos and layered styling. The look here is built around functional staples—cardigans, pencil skirts, button-downs, and V-neck sweaters—animated by the element of surprise, like a flash of red peeking out from a printed shirt, or vibrant leather gloves that contrast a more muted color palette. Recreate the [lit-chic look](https://www.vogue.com/article/literary-chic-trend) with &Daughters’s cardigan over TWP’s blue button-down and Jil Sander’s pleated skirt.

## Lady of Leisure

Driven by a sense of elegant ease, soft, draped tailoring and robe-like silhouettes define the mood—think of these as the kind of clothes Marella Agnelli might wear sailing along the Amalfi coast. Long scarves are a key styling detail, wrapped around the neck or waist, with swishy tassels that sashay with each step. Look for pieces in silk and satin that move with the body just so, like Khaite’s butter yellow skirt or Fforme’s maxi dress.

## Fashion as Feeling

There was a shared love of craft and bold use of color in the collections of Chanel, Bottega Veneta, Loewe, Balenciaga, Khaite, and Altuzarra. We saw several statement skirts paired with simple tops; the texture and dramatic proportion being the focal point of the look. Try Loulou de Saison’s fringe dress, which is still neutral yet bold, or opt for Chloé’s tiered maxiskirt with a simple knit for playful charm.

## Scarf Styling

With blown-up prints and exaggerated proportions, scarves went from accent to key element of the look. Multicolored and patterned silks were fashioned into tops and dresses, wrapped around the waist, and knotted over the shoulder for styling that pushed the limits.

# 10 Trends Set to Define Spring 2026 Fashion

Explore our deep dive into the top trends from the runways.

By [Kristen Nichols](https://www.whowhatwear.com/author/kristen-nichols), [Eliza Huber](https://www.whowhatwear.com/author/eliza-huber)

  last updated

in [Features](https://www.whowhatwear.com/features)

Spring/summer 2026 was the season of creative-director debuts and new ideas. There were upward of 15 new designers who took the helm at some of the biggest houses across the industry during the [spring/summer 2026 shows](https://www.whowhatwear.com/paris-fashion-week-trends-spring-summer-2026) with highly anticipated debut collections, including [Louise Trotter at Bottega Veneta](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/bottega-veneta-milan-fashion-week-spring-2026), [Jonathan Anderson at Christian Dior](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/dior-paris-fashion-week-spring-summer-2026), and [Matthieu Blazy at Chanel](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/chanel-paris-fashion-week-spring-summer-2026), and brought the most high-impact moments. This went in tandem with strong sophomore collections, where designers such as [Michael Rider at Celine](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/celine-paris-fashion-week-summer-2026) and [Haider Ackermann at Tom Ford](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/tom-ford-paris-fashion-week-spring-2026) continued to carefully construct and refine their visions.

Conversations in the months since have centered on the wave of change that is thrusting us into the future of fashion. It was a season about newness and challenging old ideas as designers presented collections that were doing something fresh and different. It was a palpable shift that ushered in shows that were cinematic, disruptive, bold, and optimistic. Sex appeal returned to the runway with seductive and provocative looks, off-kilter silhouettes reimagined femininity in fashion, and inventive styling—including [bold color clashing](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/color-combinations-2026) and [advanced layering](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/outfit-ideas/layering-outfit-ideas-2026)—went on to influence editorial moments, [red carpet looks](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/celebrity/red-carpet), and how the fashion set is currently getting dressed. It will go down as one of the biggest fashion months ever, marking an unprecedented moment that kick-started a new chapter in fashion. Ahead, read more on the 10 [spring 2026 trends](https://www.whowhatwear.com/paris-fashion-week-trends-spring-summer-2026) that are dominating fashion.

### The Great Debut

Debuts! Debuts! Debuts! There was an undeniable sense of newness on the runways that stemmed from the fresh creative leadership. At [Chanel](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/chanel), Blazy took this head-on. "We can go two ways," Blazy told Tim Blanks in an interview for [Business of Fashion](https://www.businessoffashion.com/articles/luxury/matthieu-blazy-launch-bof-500-class-of-2025-fashion-industry-designers/). "Either we do a clean, modern, by the codes, by the book [Chanel](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/chanel) show, and it's a first step. Or we do this show as if it was our last. I took the last option." The collection he unveiled was confident, including the finale look that embodied the joy and renewed energy we've been eager to see unfold. Though it infused the heritage and house codes from Coco Chanel, it was distinctly Blazy. This was mirrored at other debut shows too, including Anderson at [Dior](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/dior), Dario Vitale at [Versace](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/versace), Trotter at [Bottega Veneta](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/bottega-veneta), Pierpaolo Piccioli at [Balenciaga](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/balenciaga), and Jack McCollough and Lazaro Hernandez at [Loewe](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/loewe), who each brought their distinct visions to the runways.

### Sexually Explicit

If there were one collection from the entirety of the spring/summer 2026 runway season that proved, without a shadow of a doubt, that sex is back in fashion, it was Hermès, a brand known for its modest, sophisticated approach to dressing. The French house strayed from the norm this season, and Creative Director Nadège Vanhée embraced tighter and more revealing silhouettes made almost exclusively out of supple leather. Hermès wasn't the only brand to err on the risqué side this season, though. Mugler's new creative director, Miguel Castro Freitas, used his debut as a chance to do what the house has always done best: explicitly seductive tailoring. Meanwhile, Ackermann's second [Tom Ford](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/tom-ford) collection brought back the provocative look the brand's founder is known for introducing at [Gucci](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/gucci) when he took the helm in 1994. From the ambiance to the clothes, everything at Ackermann's sophomore show was daring and sensual, bringing sexy back in a way that fashion people were all too willing to give a blatant two thumbs up for.

zara

Pleated Lace Top

Massimo Dutti

Nappa Leather Miniskirt

Saint Laurent

Jill Boots

### Gone Bourgeois

A bourgeois aesthetic has been percolating in fashion. Rider kicked the aesthetic off for spring 2026 at his debut [Celine](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/celine) show in July and doubled down on it for his [summer 2026 sophomore show](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/celine-paris-fashion-week-summer-2026). Rider is distilling Left Bank Parisian style and American sportswear through a modern eye, making classic pieces like satin scarves, tailored trench coats, and colorful accessories feel cool and covetable once again. Rider has stated that his vision for [Celine](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/celine) is built on "quality for timelessness and for style," and his designs emphasize well-crafted, collectible pieces that are meant to last and be worn for the long run over ephemeral trends. This attitude toward design was mirrored on the runways at more brands with a vision of the modern investment wardrobe. At Bottega Veneta, sumptuous pastel knits were styled with suiting. At Kallmeyer, printed satin scarves were draped over sleek navy jackets. At Tom Ford and [Ralph Lauren](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/ralph-lauren), crisp white suiting interplayed with shirting and tied-up knits. It's peak good taste.

Polo Ralph Lauren

Double Breasted Knit Blazer

CELINE

Bandana

### Optimism! Joy! Delight!

With so much happening in the world around us and our phones constantly buzzing with alerts about another piece of bad news, it felt like a welcome reprieve to see fashion that felt optimistic and full of joy instead of muted and minimalist. It wasn't just bright colors, either. Those certainly did show up at Tom Ford, Jacquemus, and Chloé. Better, however, was the fresh energy overall, and floral patterns big and small mimicked the start of a new season for top houses like Chanel, Bottega Veneta, and Balenciaga. This feeling of delight was perhaps best seen at Blazy's Chanel debut when he stepped out from behind the metaphorical curtain to receive his standing ovation from the awed crowd as well as an ear-to-ear smile and hug from his first Chanel bride—played beautifully by Awar Odhiang, who donned a skirt covered in a feathery mélange that met the moment immaculately.

CHLOÉ

Lace-Trimmed Crinkled Top

By Malene Birger

Tory Burch

### Advanced Layering

While designers are presenting silhouettes and ideas that are entirely new this season, they're also challenging us to rethink how we're wearing the clothes that are already sitting in our closets. Inventive styling on the runways brought the most dialed-back staples—such as button-down shirts, pencil skirts, and gloves—together in fresh ways that breathe new life into them with layering. At Loewe, poplin shirts were worn one on top of the other in too many layers to count. At Prada, button-down shirts, sheer skirts, exposed underwear, and stackers were sandwiched together. At Versace, cardigans were tied around the waist, fanning open from where a single button was secured at the top. This will shake up personal style and open up an aspirational way to dress with advanced layering.

COS

Tailored Pima Cotton Shirt

NOUR HAMMOUR

Leather Gloves

### About the Coat

The best part about winter dressing is that you can basically wear anything underneath a really great (preferably really long) coat, and people will be none the wiser about its quality. Sweatsuit? No problem. Leggings and an oversize sweater? No one has to know. It's every fashion person's trick, one that's been in practice for years, but rarely do we see it put to use post-March. Once spring hits, our coats go into storage, and out come lighter layers and, eventually, no layers at all. For spring 2026, however, designers clearly wanted to change that, debuting hero coats with the power to become your entire outfit, even when temperatures are above freezing. At Tom Ford, apple-green and sleek burgundy trenches did the work of a whole ensemble entirely on their own, and Saint Laurent models strutted outside the Eiffel Tower in sporty yet sleek trench coats made of billowy nylon that spoke for themselves. Just add some ludicrously capacious earrings and an even larger pair of sunglasses. For a more laid-back approach, Fforme, Celine, and Bottega Veneta all served up options that had an elevated yet effortless look, all of which completely hide your outfit underneath.

TOTEME

### '80s Rewind

It seems like the rest of fashion is finally picking up what Anthony Vaccarello's been putting down at Saint Laurent for the last few seasons, which is [1980s-inspired](https://www.whowhatwear.com/eighties-fashion), *well*, everything. Think big shoulders, small waists, pussy bows, and punchy colors and patterns, ranging from loud florals to refined polka dots. The French house leaned into the era even further for spring 2026, and Vaccarello pulled from Rive Gauche archives, combining flowing fabrics and bold colors to dress "enigmatic women asserting their power," according to the show notes. Chemena Kamali and Vitale took a brighter, more optimistic approach to '80s dressing, with Kamali shifting from [Chloé](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/chloe-paris-fashion-week-spring-summer-2026)'s signature '70s boho to '80s L.A. glam and Vitale marking his debut at [Versace](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/runway/versace-milan-fashion-week-spring-2026) with a crowd-pleasing collection full of looks we can imagine a modern-day Fran Drescher wearing if she reprised her role in [*The Nanny*](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fran-drescher-the-nanny-outfit) all these years later.

Ben-Amun

Ryan Earrings

Polka Dot Blouse

### Color Clash

Color was a through line this season, often appearing with the use of joyful, optimistic shades. There wasn't one standout color but rather a rainbow of contrasting hues that were paired together in unexpected ways. Versace had a "bourgeois '80s meets '90s Miami Beach" palette that brought together colors such as lilac, cherry red, and cobalt in one look. Other looks in the Versace collection had just as wild color pairings that shouldn't work on paper but simply made sense when brought together in the outfits on the runway. This theme continued across fashion month, including at brands such as Loewe, Fendi, Prada, and Proenza Schouler, where clashing shades infused color back into the runway in a way we haven't seen in many seasons. In collections from Valentino and Tory Burch, we also saw the addition of punchy hues in a subtler way. We tracked the rise of powerful color pairing throughout the season and expect it to make one of the biggest impacts on how people will be getting dressed in 2026.

ZARA

T-Shirt

HIGH SPORT

versace

Gianni Satin Pumps

### Off-Kilter

After years of ready-to-wear collections being described as pretty, wearable, and hushed, spring/summer 2026 brought up a very different conversation. For the season, designers at Tory Burch, Prada, Khaite, and Dior appeared to be guided by the "so wrong it's right" mentality, drawing inspiration from imperfection and visual oddities as opposed to what's traditionally been considered beautiful by society. In Prada's show notes, Creative Directors Raf Simons and [Miuccia Prada](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/luxury/miuccia-prada-scarf-styling) discussed their use of "unexpected and unanticipated" elements being united on the body. They focused on liberating pieces from "seemingly inherent hierarchies," using spring 2026 as an opportunity to make people think twice about how things have always been done. "There are radical reconsiderations of the fundamental properties of clothes. Skirts find their points of suspension from the shoulder. Brassieres have shape without structure," the show notes state.

Frankenstein skirts, or garments crafted out of various opposing fabrics and cuts, became a primary example of the off-kilter nature of fashion in future seasons. At Tory Burch, the designer shared another comparable vision, noting "the complexity of women and different facets of their style" and how both result in a mix of "precision and imperfection." Clashing styles and juxtaposed aesthetics on the runway went to show how fashion is changing its approach, moving away from what women are expected to wear and toward what feels the most natural to them, which isn't always clean lines, refined styling, and muted color palettes.

Prada

Shetland Wool Skirt

Tory Burch

Clear Pump

Miu Miu

Satin Cone Bra

### A+ Prep

Preppy dressing is back. Auralee has been linked with the preppy style resurgence for the past several seasons. Its take on pieces such as polos and cable-knit sweaters is bringing a refined and modern perspective to the classic styles. At Miu Miu, collared polo shirts in shades like tangerine were styled with midi skirts and printed scarves. At Loewe, polo shirts were also prevalent, often paired with V-neck sweaters on top in contrasting colors. Tory Burch, a brand closely connected to prep and American sportswear, brought an elegant and sophisticated spin on the aesthetic. The collection was infused with inventive styling, including sports jackets with pops of bold color and layers of beaded necklaces. Perhaps the most unexpected brand to highlight the aesthetic was Prada, which featured crest-detailed polo jackets in shades such as kelly green that are primed to be a cult buy next year. It's a new lens that is bringing preppy dressing back for 2026.

Associate Editorial Director

Eliza Huber is currently the Associate Editorial Director at Who What Wear. She joined the company in 2021 as a fashion editor after starting her career as a writer at Refinery29, where she worked for four years. During her time at WWW, she launched Go Sports, the publication's sports vertical, and published four (and counting) quarterly issues tied to the WNBA, Formula One, and more. She also created two franchises, [Let's Get a Room](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/let-s-get-a-room) and [Ways to Wear](https://www.whowhatwear.com/tag/ways-to-wear); profiled [Dakota Fanning](https://www.whowhatwear.com/dakota-fanning-interview), [Diane Kruger](https://www.whowhatwear.com/diane-kruger-interview), [Katie Holmes](https://www.whowhatwear.com/katie-holmes-interview), [Gracie Abrams](https://www.whowhatwear.com/fashion/celebrity/gracie-abrams-interview), and [Sabrina Carpenter](https://www.whowhatwear.com/sabrina-carpenter-interview) for WWW's monthly cover features; and reported on new seasonal trends, up-and-coming designers, and celebrity style.
